findings.message: Destructive delete action has no confirmation step. A misclick irreversibly removes an item with no chance to recover, which is a usability hazard for irreversible operations.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the delete in a confirmation step (e.g. window.confirm or a confirm dialog/modal) before issuing the DELETE request, or offer an undo affordance after deletion.

findings.message: Mutating DELETE action has no in-flight feedback: the button is not disabled while the request is pending and shows no pending/spinner state, so users may double-click or perceive the UI as frozen on slow networks.
findings.suggestion: Track an `isPending` state, disable the button and show a spinner/'Deleting…' label while the request is in flight.

findings.message: The DELETE response is not checked and there is no error feedback path — a failed request (non-2xx or network error) silently reloads the page, leaving the user with no indication the action failed.
findings.suggestion: Inspect the response (throw on !response.ok), catch errors, and surface a user-visible error message (e.g. role="alert") instead of unconditionally reloading.

findings.message: Using window.location.reload() after a successful delete is a heavy, jarring feedback pattern — the entire app re-mounts with no success indication, which is a dead-end/confusing-flow affordance for an in-place list mutation.
findings.suggestion: Update local state (e.g. lift items into a parent and remove the deleted row, or refetch via the existing fetchItems) and show a brief success toast instead of a full page reload.
